question: When should you use embedding (nested documents) vs references in MongoDB?

options.text: Embedding -- for data read together that doesn't grow unboundedly; References -- for growing collections and independently accessed data
Always embedding, since MongoDB is a document DB and JOINs aren't needed
options.explanation: Embedding doesn't work for unboundedly growing arrays -- MongoDB documents are limited to 16 MB. If a user has thousands of orders, embedding will overflow the document

options.text: Always references, like in relational DBs -- it's more reliable
options.explanation: References require additional queries ($lookup or populate). If data is always read together (e.g., shipping address in an order), embedding is more efficient -- one query instead of two

options.text: Embedding only for primitives, references for objects
options.explanation: Data type doesn't determine the choice. You can embed entire objects (shipping address in order). The criteria are read patterns and data growth

question: Why does Redis Pub/Sub require a separate connection for the subscriber?

options.text: After subscribing, the connection enters subscription mode and cannot execute other commands (GET, SET, etc.)
For increased throughput -- two connections are faster than one
options.explanation: The reason isn't performance but the Redis protocol. In subscription mode, the client can only subscribe/unsubscribe and receive messages. Any other command returns an error

options.text: Redis doesn't support pub/sub and regular commands on the same port
options.explanation: Redis supports all commands on the same port. The restriction is at the connection level: after SUBSCRIBE, it's blocked for other commands

options.text: It's a recommendation but you can use one connection
options.explanation: It's a protocol requirement, not a recommendation. After SUBSCRIBE, the client enters subscriber mode and cannot execute GET/SET. Attempting to do so will cause an error

question: What does COLLSCAN in MongoDB's explain() indicate and how do you fix it?

options.text: COLLSCAN means full collection scan (O(n)) -- create an index on filter fields to get IXSCAN (O(log n))
COLLSCAN is normal for small collections in MongoDB
options.explanation: Even for small collections, COLLSCAN is suboptimal. MongoDB uses IXSCAN automatically when a suitable index exists. For growing collections, COLLSCAN becomes a bottleneck

options.text: COLLSCAN means an error -- the collection is corrupted
options.explanation: COLLSCAN is not an error but a scan method. It means MongoDB checks every document. It works but is slow on large collections

options.text: Restart MongoDB to recalculate query plans
options.explanation: Restarting won't help -- MongoDB chooses COLLSCAN because there's no suitable index. The solution is createIndex() on fields used in find()

question: What happens to a Redis key if you don't set a TTL?

options.text: The key lives forever until explicitly deleted -- this causes memory leaks when using Redis as a cache
Redis automatically deletes the key after 24 hours
options.explanation: Redis does NOT auto-delete keys without TTL. A key without expiration exists until explicit DEL or Redis restart (if no persistence)

options.text: The key is removed on memory overflow thanks to LRU
options.explanation: LRU eviction only works if maxmemory-policy is configured (default is noeviction). Without configuration, Redis returns OOM error. Always set TTL

options.text: Nothing bad -- Redis manages memory automatically
options.explanation: Redis doesn't magically manage memory. Without TTL and maxmemory-policy, keys accumulate indefinitely. When using as cache, always set TTL via setEx

question: What advantages do Mongoose middleware (hooks) provide?

options.text: Automatic password hashing in pre('save') -- no need to remember in every controller
Soft-delete via pre(/^find/) -- automatic filtering of deleted documents in all queries
Mongoose middleware speeds up MongoDB queries by 10x
options.explanation: Middleware doesn't affect MongoDB query performance. It executes additional logic before/after operations, adding overhead rather than speeding things up

options.text: Cascade deletion of related documents in pre('deleteOne')
